svg.text: Stoicism: founding, dormancy, and two revivals that converge
axis compressed at the ~1,350-year gap between antiquity and Neostoicism; corpus: 101 sources, c. 300 BCE–2025
Roman Stoa: ethics as practice, plus ancient testimony & critique (45 BCE–225 CE)
Neostoic revival & the critique that follows (1584–1807)
breaks from the Cynics & Academy — c. 300 BCE
logic, physics, ethics as one system — c. 230 BCE
De Finibus III, De Natura Deorum II, De Fato — 45–44 BCE
systematic doxography of Old Stoic ethics — 25 BCE
ethics-as-therapy for a Roman audience — 45–65 CE
training (askesis), not theory, makes one good — 70 CE
hostile but fragment-rich Chrysippus source — 100 CE
the dichotomy of control as training manual — 108–135 CE
a private notebook, terminal Roman Stoic text — 180 CE
skeptical & Peripatetic critiques of Stoic logic/fate — 180–200 CE
closes ancient testimony on the school — 225 CE
re-founds Stoicism for a Christian, post-civil-war readership — 1584/1589
du Vair's Morall Philosophy of the Stoicks
Stoic freedom as merely formal, “a slave's response” — 1807

transmission from Lipsius into political thought (Grotius, Hobbes, Rousseau) and into Descartes's and Spinoza's rationalism runs through this span — no single dated corpus entry, so shown as a span rather than a node
20th century: the ethics survives the physics, and splits into two lineages
(inference: framed here as a split because both lineages independently trace to the same primary texts — Epictetus, Marcus Aurelius — without citing each other until Robertson 2010)
Academic recovery of the ancient system
Stoic propositional logic as rigorous system — 1953
Hadot's Way of Life & Inner Citadel
Philosophical critique of the passions doctrine
Nussbaum's Therapy of Desire & Sorabji's Emotion and Peace of Mind
is extirpating the passions psychologically adequate? — 1994/2000
Ellis's Reason and Emotion in Psychotherapy
opens with Epictetus's maxim on judgment
Montgomery's Ancient Origins of Cognitive Therapy
bridge: names the lineage scholarly recovery had not yet cited — 2010
Convergence: the contemporary Modern Stoicism movement — tested and contested (2008–2025)
Irvine's Guide to the Good Life
Pigliucci's How to Be a Stoic
“naive” popular Stoicism linked to worse well-being — 2022
Durrant's Three Reasons Not to Be a Stoic
Cohen et al.'s RCT (2024) & LeBon et al.'s SABS (2025)
empirical tests converge: an RCT and a validated Stoicism scale
